content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 16, 2009

Jill Arthur
11702 Via Rancho
Santa Ana, CA 92705

Craig Arthur
Orange County Superior Court
341 The City Drive
P.O. Box 14169
Orange, CA 92863-1569

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our file No. A-09-222

Dear Mr. and Ms. Arthur:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance.

QUESTION

Do your positions as public officials affect your adult son's ability to accept a donation of $25,000 toward his college education from a donor with whom he has a personal relationship?

CONCLUSION

No. Your son is not considered a "dependent child" under current staff advice; the donation would therefore not be considered a gift to either of you as public officials. Please note, however, that the Commission is reconsidering the current staff-created definition of "dependent child" at its meeting on December 10, 2009.

_______________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-09-222
Page No. 2


FACTS

You are each public officials by virtue of your positions as a Court Commissioner (Mr.
Arthur) and a Santa Ana city employee (Ms. Arthur).

Your 18 year-old son has a relationship with his friend's step-father. The two boys went
to high school together, played basketball together, and were otherwise friends. Through the
course of spending time over at his friend's house, your son developed a relationship with his
friend's step-father. When the step-father learned that your son would not be able to attend
Santa Clara University past the first semester of his freshman year, he came to you and offered to
give your son $25,000 to cover the cost of the second semester.

You provided additional information, at our request, to explain that when your son is not
at school, he will live in your home and will be partially responsible for his room and board.
You also explained that you are both acquainted with the donor, but the primary relationships are
between the two boys and between your son and the donor. Additionally, we inquired as to your
son's plans should he not have this gift from the donor, and you responded that there is currently
no "back-up plan" for either this year, or the ensuing years of his college career.

ANALYSIS

Gifts

The Act requires specific public officials to disclose on a "Statement of Economic
Interest," the receipt of any gifts, from certain sources, with an aggregate value of $50 or more
received during the applicable reporting period. (Sections 87207(a)(1) and 87302(b).) Court
Commissioners, such as Mr. Arthur, are required to disclose reportable gifts from all sources.
(Sections 87200 and 87207(a)(1).) Designated employees, such as Ms. Arthur, are required to
report gifts from sources identified in their agency's conflict of interest code. (Section
87302(b).)

Section 82028 generally defines a gift as "any payment that confers a personal benefit on
the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received."
Regulation 18944 addresses gifts to an official's family members, providing that, generally, a
gift given to an official and his or her family is a gift to the official for the full value.
(Regulation 18944(a).) Additionally, a gift given to a member of an official's "immediate
family" is considered to be to the official if the official enjoys a direct benefit, unless the benefit
is nominal. (Regulation 18944(b).)

Dependent Children

Section 82029 defines "immediate family" as "the spouse and dependent children."
Through advice letters, the Commission has advised that a "dependent child" is a "natural or
adopted child who is under the age of 18 and eligible to be claimed as a dependent for tax

File No. A-09-222
Page No. 3

purposes." (See, e.g, Remcho Advice Letter, A-94-062; see also Hanley Advice Letter, A-04-
129.). While this definition is currently under review and could change as early as the
Commission's next meeting on December 10th, 2009, it remains our current advice and
applicable here. Therefore, at this time, gifts given to your son are presumed not to be gifts to
either of you.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Heather M. Rowan
Counsel, Legal Division

HMR:jgl

sections.analysis:
Gtfts
The Act requires specific public officials to disclose on a "statement of Economic
Interest," the receipt of any gifts, from certain sources, with an aggregate value of $50 or more
received during the applicable reporting period. (Sections 87207(a)(l) and 87302(b).) Court
Commissioners, such as Mr. Arthur, are required to disclose reportable gifts from all sources.
(Sections 87200 and87207(a)(1).) Designated employees, such as Ms. Arthur, are required to
report gifts from sources identified in their agency's conflict of interest code. (Section
87302(b).)
Section 82028 generally defines a gift as "any payment that confers a personal benefit on
the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received."
Regulation 18944 addresses gifts to an official's family members, providing that, generally, a
gift given to an official and his or her family is a gift to the official for the full value.
(Regulation L89M(a).) Additionally, a gift given to a member of an official's "immediate
family" is considered to be to the official if the official enjoys a direct benefit, unless the benefit
is nominal. (Regulation 18944(b).)
Dependent Children
Section 82029 defines "immediate family" as "the spouse and dependent children."
Through advice letters, the Commission has advised that a "dependent child" is a "natural or
adopted child who is under the age of 18 and eligible to be claimed as a dependent for tax
purposes." (See, e.g, Remcho Advice Letter, A-94-062; see also Hanley Advice Letter, A-04-
129.). While this definition is currently under review and could change as early as the
Commission's next meeting on December 10th, 2009, it remains our current advice and
applicable here. Therefore, at this time, gifts given to your son are presumed not to be gifts to
either of you.
